title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a festival organiser called Tom talking about a local book festival. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a festival organiser called Tom talking about a local book festival. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A festival organiser called Tom explains how young volunteers can help.
extracts.audio_script: Good afternoon. I’m Tom and I organise the Northgate Book Festival, which takes place every October. It began with three author talks in the main library, but the festival office is not in the public reading area as many people think. We organise everything from the [9]library basement[/9], an old storage space which has been repainted and equipped with desks. This year we are hoping to recruit more young volunteers, because the festival has expanded from one weekend to five full days of events at different locations around the town.||The programme includes crime writers, poets, historians and journalists. Last year tickets for travel writers sold fastest, perhaps because people were thinking about holidays again. This year, early bookings show that talks by [10]children's authors[/10] are attracting the most interest, so we need extra helpers at family events. Volunteers receive training in September. They learn about the venues and emergency procedures, but the part most people find useful is practising with the online [11]booking system[/11], since visitors often need help locating reservations or checking available seats.||During the festival, volunteers are easy to recognise. We considered badges on ordinary clothes, but they were difficult to spot in crowds. Everybody working at an event will therefore be given a [12]green T-shirt[/12] to wear while on duty. Jobs vary. Some helpers check tickets at doors, others guide visitors between buildings. A popular assignment is assisting inside the [13]poetry tent[/13], which is set up in the town park and hosts short readings throughout the afternoon. It is covered, so events continue in ordinary autumn rain, though strong wind may require a change of venue.||We value the time volunteers give and provide drinks during their shifts. Because every event is different, free tickets are difficult to organise fairly. Instead, volunteers who complete four shifts receive [14]book tokens[/14], which can be spent at festival stalls or local bookshops. There are opportunities to develop skills too. Anyone aged sixteen or over may attend a [15]writing workshop[/15] with one of our guest authors; places are limited, so volunteers should register early. Between shifts, people often meet in the [16]café[/16] on the library's ground floor, where a noticeboard displays updates and changes to event times.||Our festival also creates material for people who cannot attend. The local newspaper publishes reviews, while a team of students records short [17]interviews[/17] with authors for the festival website. This year’s profits will not be spent on extra advertising. We are working with the library to create a small outdoor [18]reading garden[/18], with benches and plants where visitors can enjoy books in good weather. Volunteers will be invited to its opening next spring. Forms listing possible shifts are available tonight, so please take one if you are interested in helping.

dialogue.speaker: tom
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I’m Tom and I organise the Northgate Book Festival, which takes place every October. It began with three author talks in the main library, but the festival office is not in the public reading area as many people think. We organise everything from the library basement, an old storage space which has been repainted and equipped with desks. This year we are hoping to recruit more young volunteers, because the festival has expanded from one weekend to five full days of events at different locations around the town.||The programme includes crime writers, poets, historians and journalists. Last year tickets for travel writers sold fastest, perhaps because people were thinking about holidays again. This year, early bookings show that talks by children's authors are attracting the most interest, so we need extra helpers at family events. Volunteers receive training in September. They learn about the venues and emergency procedures, but the part most people find useful is practising with the online booking system, since visitors often need help locating reservations or checking available seats.||During the festival, volunteers are easy to recognise. We considered badges on ordinary clothes, but they were difficult to spot in crowds. Everybody working at an event will therefore be given a green T-shirt to wear while on duty. Jobs vary. Some helpers check tickets at doors, others guide visitors between buildings. A popular assignment is assisting inside the poetry tent, which is set up in the town park and hosts short readings throughout the afternoon. It is covered, so events continue in ordinary autumn rain, though strong wind may require a change of venue.||We value the time volunteers give and provide drinks during their shifts. Because every event is different, free tickets are difficult to organise fairly. Instead, volunteers who complete four shifts receive book tokens, which can be spent at festival stalls or local bookshops. There are opportunities to develop skills too. Anyone aged sixteen or over may attend a writing workshop with one of our guest authors; places are limited, so volunteers should register early. Between shifts, people often meet in the café on the library's ground floor, where a noticeboard displays updates and changes to event times.||Our festival also creates material for people who cannot attend. The local newspaper publishes reviews, while a team of students records short interviews with authors for the festival website. This year’s profits will not be spent on extra advertising. We are working with the library to create a small outdoor reading garden, with benches and plants where visitors can enjoy books in good weather. Volunteers will be invited to its opening next spring. Forms listing possible shifts are available tonight, so please take one if you are interested in helping.
